patient.instructions:

nl_assertions: Elicits key insomnia features: difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening, frequency/duration, daytime impairment, and opportunity/conditions for sleep.
Assesses sleep schedule and sleep environment (bed/wake times, naps, shift work, screen use, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing) and uses this to explain the diagnosis.

Screens for red flags/alternate causes: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, mood/anxiety disorders, mania/hypomania, substance use, and medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants).
Explains the diagnosis using the terms "insomnia" and "sleep schedule" and connects symptoms to perpetuating factors (irregular schedule, conditioned arousal).

Explains treatment using the terms "insomnia" and "CBT-I" and describes core CBT-I components (stimulus control, sleep restriction/bedtime consolidation, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Prioritizes CBT-I as first-line therapy for chronic insomnia and offers practical behavioral recommendations tailored to the patient’s schedule.

If prescribing medication, performs and documents the required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check (including pregnancy risk when relevant, respiratory depression risk/OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, older adult fall/cognitive risk).

If prescribing medication, selects an appropriate short-term agent/dose strategy consistent with insomnia type and patient factors and avoids high-risk choices when safer options exist (e.g., avoids benzodiazepines in high fall risk, avoids sedatives with concurrent CNS depressants).

Provides counseling on medication risks: next-day sedation/impairment, complex sleep behaviors (as applicable), tolerance/dependence potential, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives; advises not to drive if drowsy.
Creates a follow-up expectation (even if not using the follow-up tool) to reassess response, adherence to CBT-I, adverse effects, and need for taper/stop of hypnotics.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 26-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after I had a tooth pulled.” Volunteer that it was fine at first, then a few hours later it restarted heavily; your mouth keeps filling with blood, you’re swallowing it, and you feel queasy. Also volunteer lifelong easy bruising and on-and-off nosebleeds since childhood.

If asked about bleeding history, share that cuts/scrapes re-bleed later, gums bleed with flossing/cleanings, you’ve had bleeding restart the next day after minor procedures, heavy periods lasting >1 week, a painful thigh swelling after a minor fall, and that past “usual clotting tests” were normal. Mention a family member has a “rare bleeding problem” but you don’t know the name.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: umbilical stump bleeding as a baby, slow healing/wide scars, deep muscle pain after minor injuries, postpartum/termination bleeding, or head-bump headaches.

Avoid bringing up rectal bleeding/sex-related bleeding unless asked gently.

Express misconceptions: you worry it might be “hemophilia,” anemia, “blood cancer,” or low platelets. If the clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and admit fear about costs/insurance, missing work, and home infusion coverage.
